nativeRange.habitat: Streams and tributaries of lowland rainforest; the Mamberamo River drainage in northern Irian Jaya (West Papua), Indonesia

decorPreferences: Dense planting at sides and back with open central swimming area
Fine-leaved plants or spawning mops for breeding
Floating plants to diffuse light and reduce skittishness
Darker substrate tends to enhance coloration

diet.notes: Generally accepts a wide range of foods including quality flakes, nano pellets, frozen cyclops, brine shrimp, daphnia, and bloodworms. Regular inclusion of live or frozen foods has been reported to intensify coloration and support conditioning for breeding.

goodWith: Amano shrimp and larger freshwater shrimp
avoidWith: Cherry shrimp and small dwarf shrimp (likely to be predated)

summary: One of the smallest rainbowfish; males display an iridescent blue body with vivid red-orange fins that intensify significantly with maturity. An excellent schooling fish for 75L+ planted community tanks.
careNotes: Among the smallest members of family Melanotaeniidae, typically reaching 5–7 cm in aquaria (FishBase records a maximum standard length of 5.0 cm, equating to roughly 6–7 cm total length). Males are considerably more vivid than females — females show a silvery body with yellowish fins. Color tends to intensify noticeably with maturity, which can take 1–2 years; young specimens purchased from shops may appear comparatively washed-out. Males display shimmering head stripes during courtship. Best kept in groups of 6 or more; a ratio of roughly 1–2 females per male has been reported to reduce male-to-male squabbling and encourage natural display behavior. An active, open-water swimmer that needs ample unobstructed swimming space despite preferring a planted setup. Tends to be somewhat skittish — floating plants to diffuse overhead light and a darker substrate are commonly recommended to settle the fish. Prefers moderately soft to moderately hard water; generally considered adaptable, though high water quality with regular partial water changes is advised by specialist sources. IUCN status: Least Concern (2019 assessment).
breedingNotes: Egg-scatterer with no parental care; spawning tends to occur among fine-leaved plants or over a spawning mop. Not considered difficult to spawn under appropriate conditions — conditioning with live or frozen foods is commonly recommended prior to breeding. Eggs generally hatch in approximately 7–10 days at typical aquarium temperatures. Fry are very small and have been reported to require microscopic foods (infusoria or commercial fry powder) initially, progressing to baby brine shrimp nauplii after 1–2 weeks. Adult fish will eat both eggs and fry, so separating eggs or using a dedicated breeding setup tends to improve survival rates.

primary.name: Aquarium Co-Op
primary.notes: Primary source for care parameters, diet, compatibility, tank sizing, and breeding overview.

additional.name: SeriouslyFish
additional.notes: Cross-checked water parameters (temp 23–28°C, pH 6.8–7.5, hardness 5–15°H), tank minimum (55L / 24" footprint), adult size (8 cm max, usually smaller in aquaria), grouping (6–8+), and breeding. Hardness range narrowed to SF's 5–15°H (more precise than AC's general 'harder water' note). pH averaged between SF (6.8–7.5) and user-brief (6.5–8.0) to give 6.5–7.8. Care level noted as 'moderate' by SF — reconciled to intermediate.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked scientific name, taxonomy (Melanotaeniidae / Atheriniformes), native range (Mamberamo River, northern Irian Jaya / West Papua, Indonesia), temperature (22–28°C), and maximum standard length (5.0 cm SL). FishBase SL values converted to approximate total length for adultSizeCm. IUCN Least Concern status confirmed.

fish.breedingNotes: Egg-scatterer; deposits eggs among fine-leaved plants or spawning mops without parental care. Spawning itself is not considered particularly difficult but raising fry is more demanding due to their very small initial size and high feeding frequency requirements.
